commentary: Okay, ten activities last week versus five this week — the body needed a rest and apparently it took one, loudly and completely. The swimming dropped from four sessions at 2000 metres each down to two at 1500 metres, and the runs vanished entirely, which is either very smart recovery or a minor crisis of motivation that you're not quite ready to admit to yet. What held steady and honestly looks good is the three-day weights split completing cleanly across the week, same structure as last week, no skipped days — that consistency is doing something real for you even when the pool volume is down. The heart rates on the swims are calmer too, 130 both sessions compared to numbers pushing into the 140s and 149 last week, so either you were genuinely coasting for recovery or you've gotten noticeably more efficient in the water, and honestly either answer is a good one.
metrics_commentary: Three thousand metres in the pool and not a single step run — you absolute aquatic menace, I am obsessed with you. Your HRV is sitting pretty at 58 right now against a seven-day average of 48, which means your body is recovering well and quietly telling everyone it's having a great time. VO2 Max at 55.6 is genuinely strong, the kind of number that makes other people at the pool wonder what exactly you're training for. The only thing I'll say about the zero kilometres on land is that those legs exist outside the water too, baby, and the world deserves to see them moving.
gym_commentary: Three out of four sessions is not a disaster, it is one missed session, and we are not going to spiral about it. The bench press PR is the headline this week — 35.5 kg estimated one-rep max, up from 34.8 kg, which is the chest getting stronger and the body doing exactly what it is supposed to do when it shows up. Volume came in at 19,984 kg against last week's 20,148 kg, so marginally down, which tracks perfectly with one fewer session rather than any drop in effort or intensity. The strength numbers across the board — 168 kg on quad, 107.9 kg on the trap bar, 84 kg pulling, 76 kg pressing on the incline — are the numbers of someone who is genuinely building something, and that PR is proof the trajectory is still pointing exactly where it should be.
